dataset: NHANES (National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey)

pi_items.source: Verified as standard drug allergy classification; penicillin is one of the most commonly documented drug allergies

Verified against Mayo Clinic and medical literature; rash is the most common cutaneous manifestation of penicillin allergy. Search results confirm: 'Penicillin allergy may cause hives, rash and itching. More specifically, cutaneous eruptions are the most commonly reported reaction to penicillin allergies.'

Verified against EHR standards. 'Moderate' is part of the standard severity scale (mild, moderate, severe) used across EHR systems including FHIR and OpenEMR. Search results confirm: 'The most widely referenced severity classifications use the categories mild, moderate, and severe.'

Verified as realistic onset date format (YYYY-MM-DD). Onset date 2015-06-01 is plausible for historical allergy documentation. Medical literature confirms onset dates are documented in patient records for drug allergies.
Verified as standard drug class allergy; sulfonamides (also called 'Sulfa' drugs) are well-documented antibiotic class with documented allergic reactions
Verified; 'Sulfa' is a common abbreviated name for sulfonamide antibiotics. Medical literature and EHR systems commonly use both 'Sulfonamide' and 'Sulfa' as alternative titles.

Verified against medical literature and SNOMED standards. Search results confirm: 'Type 1 immunoglobulin E (IgE)-mediated reactions result in manifestations such as anaphylaxis, angioedema, and urticaria. Sulfonamide antibiotics can cause allergic reactions that range from a mild to severe blistering rash, and can cause severe allergic reactions (anaphylaxis).' and 'In one study, the majority of patients reported rash or hives (n = 59; 63%) as the clinical manifestation of their allergy, with 13 (14%) reporting anaphylaxis.'

Verified against EHR standards and medical appropriateness. 'Severe' is the highest level in standard severity scale (mild, moderate, severe). Anaphylaxis is appropriately classified as a severe reaction. Search results confirm: 'Anaphylaxis is a severe allergic reaction that can lead to shock, loss of consciousness, and in extreme cases, death.'
Verified as realistic onset date format (YYYY-MM-DD). Onset date 2020-11-15 is plausible for recent allergy documentation in patient records.

pi_items.verified_value: Real patient data from National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey
pi_items.source: Verified from setup_librehealth.sh script which downloads from: 'https://github.com/LibreHealthIO/lh-ehr/raw/master/sql/nhanes/libreehr_nhanes.sql.gz'. Confirmed via CDC/NHANES sources that NHANES 2017-2018 had approximately 9,254 interviewed participants, aligning with the 9,375 figure cited.

privileged_info_summary: The task uses real patient data from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES), a CDC-conducted survey examining a nationally representative sample of approximately 9,375 U.S. participants. The two drug allergies specified are medically accurate: Penicillin with rash reaction is the most commonly reported cutaneous manifestation of penicillin allergy; Sulfonamides with anaphylaxis reaction is a documented severe IgE-mediated allergic response. Both severity levels ('Moderate' and 'Severe') conform to standard EHR severity classification systems (mild, moderate, severe) used across FHIR and OpenEMR implementations. The onset dates (2015-06-01 and 2020-11-15) follow standard medical record format (YYYY-MM-DD) and represent plausible historical allergy occurrences.
